posts.title: Typography
posts.content:
_A field guide to the type scale, rhythm, and small details that make reading on the web feel effortless._

Typography is the quiet architecture of reading. When it works, you don't notice it — you just keep going.

This post is a compact Markdown compatibility sample for the minimal starter.

## Headings establish hierarchy

A page without hierarchy is a page without a map. Headings tell the eye where to land, and where it can safely skip ahead.

### A smaller heading, for sub-sections

Three levels of heading is usually enough. Anything deeper and you're probably writing an outline, not an essay.

## Inline rhythm

Inline elements should feel native to the paragraph: a link to [ZeroPress](https://zeropress.dev/), emphasis with *italics*, strong emphasis with **bold text**, inline code like `useState`, and a short correction with ~~deleted text~~.

> Typography is what language looks like.
>
> — Ellen Lupton

## Lists and tasks

Three things make a list useful:

- Items that are genuinely parallel.
- Short phrases, not full paragraphs.
- A reason to be a list at all.

A small checklist renders as disabled task inputs:

- [x] Draft the piece.
- [x] Revise the rhythm.
- [ ] Publish when it is ready.

Ordered lists earn their numbers when sequence matters:

1. Write the draft.
2. Cut a third of it.
3. Read it out loud.
4. Cut a third again.

## Tables

| Element | Use |
| --- | --- |
| H2 | Major sections |
| H3 | Supporting sections |
| Paragraph | Long-form reading |
| Code | Technical notes |

## Alerts

> [!NOTE]
> Notes carry neutral context that helps the reader keep moving.

> [!TIP]
> Tips suggest a practical next step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Important blocks point at details that should not be missed.

> [!WARNING]
> Warnings call out risk before the reader acts.

> [!CAUTION]
> Cautions are for the sharp edges of a workflow.

## Code fences

Even a writing-focused blog occasionally needs a snippet:

```ts
function greet(name: string) {
  return `Hello, ${name}.`;
}

greet("reader");
```

Mermaid remains a code block for theme-owned progressive enhancement:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  markdown[Markdown] --> render[ZeroPress render]
  render --> html[Static HTML]
```

---

## Rhythm is everything

Spacing is typography too. The vertical rhythm between paragraphs, the air around a heading, the pause of a horizontal rule — these are the punctuation of the page.

Get those right, and the words almost read themselves.
posts.excerpt: A field guide to the type scale, rhythm, and small details that make reading on the web feel effortless.

posts.title: On writing less
posts.content:
_The shortest essays often take the longest to write. A note on restraint, revision, and the quiet power of leaving things out._

There is a particular kind of pleasure in deleting a sentence you spent an hour writing. It feels wasteful at first — then, almost immediately, like relief.

Most of what we write is scaffolding. It exists so the rest of the building can stand. Once the walls hold, the scaffolding has to come down, or the thing you built will never be seen.

The hardest paragraphs to cut are the ones that are merely good. Bad writing announces itself; good writing hides. It earns its place by being competent, and competence is enough to survive most edits.

But a piece of writing is not the sum of its competent parts. It is the shape they make together. And shape, almost always, comes from absence.
posts.excerpt: The shortest essays often take the longest to write. A note on restraint, revision, and the quiet power of leaving things out.

posts.title: A quiet morning in Kyoto
posts.content:
_Notes from a week of walking, looking, and drinking too much coffee in a city that rewards slowness._

I arrived in Kyoto on a Tuesday and didn't speak to anyone for almost two days. Not by design — just by the friction of a new place, where every small interaction requires a little more attention than you have to spare.

The mornings were the best part. I'd walk along the Kamo river before the cyclists arrived, when the light was still grey and the herons stood in the shallows like they were waiting for something specific.

Cities reveal themselves at this hour. Without traffic, without crowds, you can hear a place breathe.
posts.excerpt: Notes from a week of walking, looking, and drinking too much coffee in a city that rewards slowness.

posts.title: Tools I actually use
posts.content:
After years of trying every new app, I keep coming back to the same handful. Here's the short list.

This post uses document_type: html. ZeroPress sanitizes the trusted body shape before it reaches the theme.

Plain HTML can still be structured

Use headings, paragraphs, links, lists, and tables when a generator already has HTML content.

  • A plain text file for notes.
  • A paper notebook for sketches.
  • A calendar that does not try to become a philosophy.
ToolReason
NotebookLow friction
Text filePortable
posts.excerpt: After years of trying every new app, I keep coming back to the same handful. Here's the short list.

posts.title: What the garden taught me
posts.content:
_A first season of growing vegetables, and the small lessons that arrived along with the tomatoes._

I grew six tomato plants last summer. Three thrived, two were average, one died for reasons I never figured out.

The garden does not care about my schedule. It does not care that I forgot to water on Thursday, or that I was traveling for the weekend. It responds to what I do, not what I intend.
posts.excerpt: A first season of growing vegetables, and the small lessons that arrived along with the tomatoes.

posts.title: The case for boring software
posts.content:
Novelty is overrated. Most of the time, the right tool is the one that has been quietly working for ten years.

This post uses document_type: plaintext. ZeroPress escapes the content and turns separated paragraphs into simple paragraph elements.

Boring software is software you don't have to think about. It does the thing, it does it the same way it did yesterday, and it will do it the same way tomorrow.
posts.excerpt: Novelty is overrated. Most of the time, the right tool is the one that has been quietly working for ten years.

pages.title: About
pages.content:
Margin is a small journal of essays and notes — written slowly, published when finished.

## Why this site

There is enough fast writing in the world. This is the slow kind: short pieces, revised more times than they probably deserved, kept around because they still felt true on the third read.

## How it's made

Drafted by hand, transcribed on quiet mornings, set in Fraunces and Inter. No tracking, no popups, no urgency.
